claimed_contribution: Integrates scarcity/price-sensitivity research with stigmatized-identity cue and discrimination literatures to explain when low-SES consumers forgo financially advantageous options.
Extends the ghetto tax literature by proposing a psychological ghetto tax: low-SES consumers may deliberately avoid cheaper options to reduce expected SES-based discrimination.
Provides evidence in both hypothetical and consequential decisions and tests a managerially actionable intervention (store inclusion statements) to mitigate the effect.
context: The studies are set in Rio de Janeiro, a highly unequal megacity where affluent neighborhoods and favelas are geographically close and low- and high-SES consumers often encounter each other in commercial spaces. Participants were recruited in wealthy areas (e.g., South Zone) and deprived areas (e.g., Favela da Mare), plus one lower-middle-income neighborhood.
design_and_data: The paper reports five studies conducted in Portuguese in Rio de Janeiro, plus pilot and pretests. Designs include mixed and between-subject experiments comparing low- vs. high-SES consumers (SES inferred by neighborhood in most studies; manipulated in Study 2) and intergroup vs. intragroup shopping contexts. Outcomes are choice-based measures of price sensitivity (e.g., choosing cheaper but less convenient options), with both hypothetical scenarios and consequential tasks using voucher lotteries or real-money purchase decisions. Mechanism evidence comes from expected discrimination measures and mediation analyses, and Study 5 manipulates inclusion-statement types (equality, diversity, antidiscrimination, control).
main_findings: Low-SES participants, relative to high-SES participants, reported greater concern with both price and potential discrimination/discomfort in upscale commercial settings.

When the cheaper option required intergroup contact (e.g., upscale or outside-neighborhood setting), low-SES consumers were less likely to choose it, while high-SES consumers were largely unaffected by group-contact context.

Expected discrimination partially explained the reduced price sensitivity among low-SES consumers, and inclusion statements emphasizing customer equality or diversity increased low-SES choice of the cheaper intergroup option (antidiscrimination statements were weaker).
one_line_summary: Across five studies in Rio de Janeiro, the paper shows that expected SES-based discrimination makes low-SES consumers less price sensitive in intergroup upscale settings, and that inclusion cues can partially restore price-sensitive choices.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior scarcity and ghetto-tax research by showing that beyond structural barriers, expected SES-based discrimination can itself reduce low-SES consumers' price sensitivity, and by documenting this effect in consequential purchase contexts with a mitigation intervention based on inclusion statements.
research_gap: Prior work had not directly shown that stigmatized-identity cues in commercial settings can make consumers incur real financial costs by becoming less price sensitive, especially among highly constrained low-SES consumers. The scarcity literature also had not examined expected discrimination as a factor that can alter the SES-price sensitivity relationship.
research_question: How do financial scarcity and expected SES-based discrimination jointly shape price sensitivity among low-SES consumers? Specifically, do low-SES consumers forgo cheaper options when those options are in intergroup upscale settings, and can store inclusion statements reduce this avoidance and restore price sensitivity?
